skill.name: routeros-command-tree
skill.description: Helps inspect and model the RouterOS command tree with the /console/inspect REST API, including paths, commands, arguments, and schema generation patterns.
skill.summary: Map RouterOS command paths, inspect API responses, and schema generation rules.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but manual review found a documentation-only skill with no executable files. Most hits are Markdown false positives; the real residual risk is operational because examples make authenticated RouterOS inspect requests and document crash-prone syntax paths that must be skipped.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated RouterOS Inspect Requests Require Care
medium_findings.description: The skill documents fetch-based POST calls to /rest/console/inspect and recursive tree traversal. This is legitimate RouterOS automation guidance, but it can send many authenticated requests to a live router and the skill notes that syntax lookup for certain argument paths can crash the RouterOS REST server if not skipped.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls and crash-prone path warning are explicitly documented. The context is legitimate device introspection, not hidden exfiltration or malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many backtick-delimited terms as Ruby or shell execution. Reviewed locations are Markdown inline code, tables, and fenced examples describing RouterOS CLI paths, REST verbs, and data shapes, not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown prose, tables, or code examples. No Ruby code, shell command invocation, or executable script file exists in this skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography hits do not correspond to crypto APIs or hash implementations. The cited context uses ordinary documentation terms such as description, syntax, and data fields inside RouterOS examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic operations in SKILL.md. The detections are keyword matches inside explanatory RouterOS documentation.

low_findings.title: Relative Link Flag Is Not Filesystem Access
low_findings.description: The path traversal finding points to a Markdown reference link using ../ to another local skill document. It is not code that reads, writes, or resolves filesystem paths from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is a Markdown link in an Additional Resources section. There is no executable filesystem API usage at that location.

low_findings.title: INSPECTFILE Mention Is Benign Configuration Guidance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance hit is a reference to using an INSPECTFILE environment variable for cached RouterOS inspect data. It does not enumerate host files, environment secrets, or system details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a performance recommendation for offline parsing. No code reads environment variables or transmits their values.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated RouterOS Inspect Requests Require Care
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents fetch-based POST calls to /rest/console/inspect and recursive tree traversal. This is legitimate RouterOS automation guidance, but it can send many authenticated requests to a live router and the skill notes that syntax lookup for certain argument paths can crash the RouterOS REST server if not skipped.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network calls and crash-prone path warning are explicitly documented. The context is legitimate device introspection, not hidden exfiltration or malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: RouterOS command trees are hard to inspect and convert into schemas by hand. This skill explains /console/inspect patterns for mapping paths, commands, arguments, and REST methods.
actual_capabilities: Explains RouterOS command tree node types and path hierarchy.
Shows how to call /rest/console/inspect for child and syntax requests.
Maps common RouterOS CLI commands to REST API verbs and URL patterns.
Describes how inspect data can become RAML or OpenAPI schemas.
Identifies RouterOS scripting paths where syntax lookups should be skipped.
limitations: Does not include executable tooling or a complete schema generator.
Requires access to a RouterOS 7.x device for live inspection workflows.
Does not validate credentials, permissions, or device reachability.
RouterOS command trees can differ by version and installed packages.

use_cases.title: Build RouterOS Schema Tools
use_cases.description: Generate REST schema resources from command tree nodes and argument descriptions.

use_cases.title: Understand Inspect Responses
use_cases.description: Interpret child, syntax, highlight, and completion responses from /console/inspect.

use_cases.title: Plan Safe Tree Traversal
use_cases.description: Avoid known syntax lookup paths that can disrupt the RouterOS REST server.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Command Path
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how the RouterOS command path /ip/address/add appears in the command tree and REST API.

prompt_templates.title: Map CLI to REST
prompt_templates.prompt: Map the RouterOS commands add, set, remove, get, print, and export to REST methods and URL patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Design Inspect Traversal
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a safe traversal plan for /console/inspect that collects children and argument syntax without querying dangerous paths.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Schema Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a plan to convert RouterOS inspect tree data into RAML and OpenAPI resources, including command and argument handling.

output_examples.input: Explain what a RouterOS cmd node means.
output_examples.output: A cmd node represents an executable RouterOS action, such as add, set, print, remove, get, or export.

output_examples.input: How should I inspect argument descriptions safely?
output_examples.output: Fetch child nodes first, then request syntax for arguments only when their path does not include dangerous scripting segments.

output_examples.input: What is unusual about RouterOS REST create operations?
output_examples.output: RouterOS commonly uses PUT to create resources with add, while PATCH updates existing resources with set.

best_practices: Use cached inspect data during development to avoid repeated router queries.
Skip syntax lookups for documented dangerous scripting path segments.
Generate schemas per RouterOS version because command trees can change.
anti_patterns: Do not assume RouterOS REST verbs follow common REST conventions.
Do not run full tree traversal repeatedly against production routers.
Do not query syntax for every argument without applying dangerous path filters.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps explain RouterOS command tree inspection and schema generation from /console/inspect data.

faq.question: Does it execute commands on my router?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation, but it includes examples for authenticated RouterOS REST requests.

faq.question: Which RouterOS versions are covered?
faq.answer: The skill describes RouterOS 7.x behavior and notes that command trees vary by version and packages.

faq.question: Can it generate OpenAPI files directly?
faq.answer: No. It explains the mapping rules and data model used by schema generators.

faq.question: Why are some paths called dangerous?
faq.answer: The skill states that syntax lookups for certain scripting argument paths can crash the RouterOS REST server.

faq.question: Can I use offline inspect data?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill describes using cached inspect data through an INSPECTFILE workflow.
